food_subgroup: Beef and tofu bowl with salad

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for low-carb gyudon with tofu substitution. FOODON_00002809 (beef bowl food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Beef dish) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for low-carb gyudon with tofu rice substitute.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific low-carb Japanese beef bowl preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising simmered beef and onion over a tofu base with a side salad dressed with sesame dressing, as served at Sukiya Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) nutrient profile — component estimation from USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for low-carb gyudon with tofu substitution exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated by component analysis: simmered beef (USDA FDC #174032 — beef, short loin, cooked), firm tofu (USDA FDC #172475), mixed salad greens (USDA FDC #169248), sesame dressing (USDA FDC #171028), onion simmered in soy-mirin broth. Proportions estimated as: beef ~120 g, tofu ~100 g, onion/broth ~80 g, salad ~50 g, dressing ~30 g per 380 g serving. Per-100 g values derived from weighted average of components. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — tofu and low-carbohydrate mixed beef dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for low-carb gyudon. GI estimated at 32 based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: firm tofu (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database) and soy-mirin broth with onion (GI ~45 for onion-based sauces). Weighted average across the carbohydrate fraction of the dish yields an estimated GI of approximately 32 (low category). Glycemic load per 380 g serving = (32 × 4.2 × 380) / (100 × 100) = 5.1 g. This is a low-GL meal. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and mirin broth
hypertension.guidance: Each 380 g serving delivers approximately 1178 mg sodium, which represents roughly 51% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content due to tofu rice substitution
type2_diabetes.guidance: The replacement of rice with tofu reduces the carbohydrate load to approximately 16 g per serving (GI ~32, GL ~5), making this a suitable meal choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who require controlled postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and moderate sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 9 g saturated fat per serving from simmered beef; individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of total saturated fat intake and consider limiting frequency of consumption or choosing leaner beef cuts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low Carb Gyudon is a Japanese-style beef bowl in which the traditional steamed white rice base is replaced with firm tofu, significantly reducing the carbohydrate content of the dish. The beef component consists of thinly sliced beef simmered with onion in a sweet-savoury broth made from dashi, soy sauce, and mirin — the classic gyudon flavour profile. The tofu base absorbs the broth and provides a soft, silken texture that contrasts with the tender beef. The dish is served with a side of mixed salad greens dressed with sesame dressing, adding freshness, crunch, and additional micronutrients. As served at Sukiya Singapore (Funan outlet), this is a commercially available low-carbohydrate adaptation of one of Japan's most popular fast-food dishes. Nutritionally, the tofu substitution reduces carbohydrates to approximately 16 g per 380 g serving compared to over 60 g in a standard gyudon, while maintaining a high protein content of approximately 37 g per serving from both beef and tofu. The glycemic index is estimated at 32 (low), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose levels. Sodium content is moderate-to-high due to the soy-mirin broth, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for non-vegetarian halal-unverified diets. It is a good source of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and calcium from tofu.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for low-carb gyudon with tofu substitution exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were estimated by component analysis using USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef, firm tofu, salad greens, and sesame dressing. GI was estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database values for tofu and onion-based sauces. Confidence is medium due to estimation methodology and absence of a direct source match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for estimation (USDA FoodData Central entries for beef, tofu, and salad greens do not consistently report vitamin D). All other -1 fields have been replaced with estimated values where component data was available. Nutrient values are per-100 g estimates derived from a 380 g serving composition of approximately 120 g cooked beef, 100 g firm tofu, 80 g onion-soy-mirin broth, 50 g mixed salad greens, and 30 g sesame dressing. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and mirin in the broth; actual restaurant sodium may vary. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included 'Japanese Curry' and 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' which are not relevant to this dish and have been disregarded as field survey platform tagging artefacts. The food_name '130. Low Carb Gyudon' has been canonicalised to 'Low Carb Gyudon' by removing the menu numbering prefix.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed menu number prefix '130.' from food_name to produce canonical English name.
Added Japanese local name '低糖質牛丼' and romanized form 'Teitōshitsu Gyūdon' to satisfy G6 Japanese cuisine requirement.
Estimated per-100 g nutrient values by component analysis using USDA FoodData Central approved sources.
Estimated GI at 32 using University of Sydney GI Database analogous values for tofu and onion-based sauces.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 with explicit sentinel value justification in notes field.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Disregarded irrelevant field survey cuisine_tags (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers) as platform artefacts.
Set ontology match types to 'closest_parent' or 'none' only — no invented codes.
